coredata.dc:description: Contaminants from antibiotic residues in milk are a potential concern for the global dairy market. We needed rapid, simple, sensitive, and selective analytical methods to ensure the safety of raw milk and milk products for customers. Here, we developed a rapid and simple competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA) using a CDs-conjugated antibody as an effective fluorescent reporter for sensitive neomycin detection in milk. Under the optimum conditions, the detection time of the proposed assay was completed in 30 min and exhibited a wide working range of 0.02–2 ng mL⁻¹ , a low detection limit (0.01 ng mL⁻¹), high precision (%RSD 2.6), and good selectivity in neomycin detection. Using this assay, neomycin levels in milk samples were easily measured in a range from 0.23 to 1.44 µg kg−1, and the recovery rates were determined to be in the range of 84.7 %–113.2 % with an acceptable RSD (3.36–10.67 %). The competitive CDs-LISA demonstrated greater analytical performance than standard immunoassays using horseradish peroxidase and showed significant promise for simple and rapid detection immunoassays, indicating the utility of this analytical method for monitoring antibiotic contamination in milk and its application to dairy food control.

TEM image of synthesized CDs (inset shows the size of synthesized CDs).

The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs and ultraviolet−visible light (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum. The red line on the left y-axis represents the UV-Vis absorption spectrum for CDs. The blue line represents the fluorescence emission spectrum on the right y-axis. Inset images display the color of solid CDs after preparation, an aqueous solution under white light, and an aqueous solution under UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm.

The CDs-conjugated antibody identified by UV-Vis (ultraviolet-visible) and fluorescence spectroscopy. (a) UV absorption spectra of pure CDs (red line) and CDs conjugated antibody (blue line). (b) fluorescence emission spectra of pure CDs (red line) and CDs conjugated antibody (blue line).
Resultant fluorescence emission spectra of immobilized neomycin_BSA with (black line) and without (red line) the presence of neomycin, and immobilized BSA only (blue line).

Optimization of experimental condition of the proposed assay. Effect of concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate (a), incubation time (b), pH (c) and binding time (d) on competitive CDs-LISA fluorescence response for detection of neomycin. The error bars were obtained from triplicate experiments.

(a) Fluorescence spectra with different concentrations of neomycin (b) Calibration curve of the fluorescence intensities versus different concentrations of neomycin (inset: representative of a linear regression). The error bars were obtained from triplicate experiments.

(a) Selectivity test results from the proposed assay toward neomycin compared with other interferent antibiotics and (b) Long-term stability test of the proposed assay by measuring the fluorescence intensities for detection of neomycin at time intervals of 90 day. The neomycin concentrations were 0.2 ng mL−1. The error bars were obtained from triplicate experiments.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive determination of neomycin residues in milk samples using a fast, simple competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Contaminants from antibiotic residues in milk are a potential concern for the global dairy market. We needed rapid, simple, sensitive, and selective analytical methods to ensure the safety of raw milk and milk products for customers. Here, we developed a rapid and simple competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA) using a CDs-conjugated antibody as an effective fluorescent reporter for sensitive neomycin detection in milk. Under the optimum conditions, the detection time of the proposed assay was completed in 30 min and exhibited a wide working range of 0.02–2 ng mL⁻¹ , a low detection limit (0.01 ng mL⁻¹), high precision (%RSD 2.6), and good selectivity in neomycin detection. Using this assay, neomycin levels in milk samples were easily measured in a range from 0.23 to 1.44 µg kg−1, and the recovery rates were determined to be in the range of 84.7 %–113.2 % with an acceptable RSD (3.36–10.67 %). The competitive CDs-LISA demonstrated greater analytical performance than standard immunoassays using horseradish peroxidase and showed significant promise for simple and rapid detection immunoassays, indicating the utility of this analytical method for monitoring antibiotic contamination in milk and its application to dairy food control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Aminoglycoside antibiotics are chemical substances produced by microorganisms that prevent the growth of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (Schwarz et al., 2001). Neomycin is extensively used in veterinary medicine to treat bacterial infections in animals, particularly bovine and goat mastitis, leading to the presence of neomycin residues in milk (Glinka et al., 2020; Jospe-Kaufman et al., 2020; Van Duijkeren et al., 2019). Milk is one of the most widely consumed foods in the world. As a result, the presence of antibiotics in milk raises several public health concerns (Liu et al., 2021; Ogier, et al., 2020; Rosenberg et al., 2020). To address these issues, national and international organizations have established maximum residue limits (MRLs) for neomycin residues in milk. For example, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has set an MRLs of 150 µg kg−1 for neomycin in milk (Luan et al., 2020). Consequentially, the rapid, sensitive, and selective assay can help manufacturers monitor neomycin residual levels in their products to ensure safe use.

To date, several analytical methods have been developed for determining neomycin residues in milk, including traditional microbial growth inhibition assays (Goffová et al., 2012; Sierra et al., 2009), spectroscopy (Tzanavaras and Themelis, 2007), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Ianni et al., 2018), liquid chromatography mass-spectrometer (LC-MS) (Msagati and Nindi, 2005; Tasci et al., 2021; Zu et al., 2018), electrochemical technique (Feng et al., 2019; Giang, 2021; F. Li et al., 2022; J. Li et al., 2022; Lian et al., 2013; Wei and Wang, 2011) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (García-Campaña et al., 2009). These methods are time-consuming and require complex and expensive laboratory equipment operated by trained staff. Thus, some alternative analytical methods, such as immunoassays (Xiao et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2012) and biosensors (Karacaglar et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2013), are increasingly being developed. The above methods have a significant advantage in terms of simple preparation, high selectivity due to the specific interaction between biomolecules, and rapid detection with low detection limits (Cheubong et al., 2023; He et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015; C. Zhang et al., 2020; X. Zhang et al., 2020). Among the various kinds, competitive ELISA, the most popular method for detecting small molecules in food analysis (Jiang et al., 2018; Merola et al., 2014; Teepoo et al., 2019), is commonly used to detect antibiotic residues in milk products due to its high specificity and sensitivity (Chen et al., 2007; Giang and Dao, 2020; Haasnoot et al., 1999; Jin et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2011). However, these methods are time-consuming, and conjugated enzymes are expensive and have low stability. Therefore, it is critical to develop a quicker, simpler, more stable, cost-effective, and sensitive method of detecting neomycin residues in milk samples.

Fluorescent CDs have been widely synthesized and utilized in various fields, such as bio-imaging and sensing (Chowmasundaram et al., 2023; Gholipour and Rahmani, 2023) because they have much more excellent properties, including unique optical properties, good fluorescence characteristics, stability, spherical shape, high solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and simple surface modifications, compared to bioprobes (enzyme and DNA) or organic fluorescent dyes (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2012; Tao et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011). These properties make fluorescent CDs stable, sensitive, and cost-effective candidates for fluorescent nanoprobes (Liu et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2022). Nowadays, the CDs-based fluorescence assays have been increasingly developed for the detection of macromolecules and small molecules in food analysis (Qi et al., 2023; Setianto et al., 2024). Using CDs-conjugated antibody as a nanoprobe is an effective key role to amplify the signal and increase the sensitivity for detection of target molecules in immunoassays. Some previous reports indicated that CDs-conjugated antibodies were most promising as fluorescent nanoprobes for application in protein and food contaminants detection (Wu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020). From our best knowledge, fluorescent CDs conjugated antibody was a small used as a fluorescent nanoprobe of competitive CDs-LISA for antibiotic detection (Song et al., 2015), and has not been reported yet to be used for neomycin detection in milk. This inspired us to explore a simple, rapid, and sensitive competitive CDs-LISA for detection of neomycin residues in milk using a CDs-conjugated antibody as an effective fluorescent reporter with increased signal response and decreased detection time compared to traditional competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), which used horseradish peroxidase.

Herein, we present a fast, simple competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA) for sensitive detection of neomycin residues in milk. First, the CDs were covalently bound to the neomycin antibody by amide coupling as an effective fluorescent reporter. After immobilization of neomycin bovine serum albumin conjugate (neomycin-BSA conjugate) used as an analyze competitor on the polystyrene well plates, the unbound neomycin-BSA was then washed using 1 % BSA (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). Next, the CDs-conjugated anti-neomycin (CDs-Abneo) was incubated with free neomycin in solutions (or samples) and then introduced into neomycin-coated wells. After competition binding between free neomycin and neomycin-BSA for CDs-Abneo binding, the fluorescence intensities of neomycin in solutions showed competitive concentration-depending binding to the CDs-Abneo captured on the well plates, such that the fluorescence intensities gradually decreased with the increasing neomycin concentration ( Fig. 1a). Then, the analytical performances of the developed assay, such as sensitivity, selectivity, accuracy, stability, and recovery, as well as the feasibility of detecting neomycin residues in real milk samples, were evaluated and compared to the competitive ELISA (Fig. 1b). Finally, the proposed assay without using bioprobes was successfully applied to detect neomycin residues in milk samples with a simple, rapid and cost-effective method.

A polyclonal anti-neomycin from rabbit was purchased from Bio Rad, USA. Neomycin sulfate, streptomycin sulfate, gentamycin sulfate, penicillin sulfate, and tetracyclin sulfate, Bovine serum albumin, EDC 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimine hydrochloride, NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide), and urea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Sodium di-hydrogen orthophosphate, di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate, sodium carbonate, sodium hydrogen carbonate, and citric acid were purchased from Ajax Finechem, Australia. Ethyl acetate was purchased from RCI Labscan, TH. Sodium hydroxide pellet was procured from Kemaus, Australia. Chloroacetic acid was purchased from LOBA Chemie, India. Polypropylene microplates and dialysis tubing (size 1 kDa and 10 kDa) were procured from Thermo scientific, USA. All chemicals were analytical grade. Fluorescence spectrophotometer, FMP-825 fluorescence microplate reader with FP-8350/8550, Jasco, Japan. UV–visible spectrophotometers, SHIMATSU, Japan. Hydrothermal reactor, Memment, Germany. Tetrafluoroethylene-Teflon-Lined Autoclave, Baoshishan, USA. Freeze Dryer, FD-3E. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) JEM-1400, JEOL Ltd. was supplied by Musa Shino Akishima, Tokyo, Japan. The zeta potentials and particle sizes were analyzed using a Malvern Zetasizer Ultra (Malvern Panalytical, United Kingdom). Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet is 5/iD7 ATR spectrometer in the range of 4000–500 cm− 1 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA). The pH values were recorded on a Denver Ultra Basic model UB-10 Benchtop pH Meter,(IL, USA).

The CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. The procedures were modified from a previous report (Qi et al., 2023). Briefly, urea 1.8 g and citric acid 2.1 g were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The colorless solution was transferred into 100 mL teflon-lined autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160 °C for 8 h, then the mixture was cooled down at room temperature. The CDs solution was filtered by membrane pore size 0.22 µm and dialyzed cellulose tubing MW cutoff:1 kDa with deionized water for 48 h. After that, the purified CDs solution was freeze dried and stored under the dark and dry conditions at room temperature for using further.

The method for conjugation of neomycin with BSA was slightly modified from a previous report (Chen et al., 2007). In detail, neomycin (50 mg) and BSA (20 mg) were dissolved in 2 mL of 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 6.5). Next, EDC (20 mg) in 1 mL of 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 6.5) was added and stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Finally, the solution was then dialyzed using cellulose tubing MW cutoff:10 kDa in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 6.5) and also shaked for 72 h at 4°C. The concentration of Neomycin-BSA conjugate was diluted in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and kept at 4 °C until needed to use.

The CDs-conjugated antibody was performed according to a previous report with slight modifications (Disha et al., 2022). Before using anti-neomycin, the sodium azide must be removed by dialysis with cellulose tubing (MW cutoff:10 kDa) in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at 4 °C for 24 h. The labeling CDs with anti-neomycin was a fresh prepared using EDC, and NHS (ratio 1:1) as amide coupling reagents, then the CDs and coupling reagents were dissolved in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. Next, the anti-neomycin was diluted to 0.1 mg mL−1 and added. The mixture was successive the reaction farther 1 h and isolated by dialysis (MW cutoff:10 kDa) in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) for 24 h to remove the excessive CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA)
ce:section.ce:para: First, the coating reagent neomycin-BSA was added into each well on a cleaned dark microplate. Then, 0.1 moL L−1 of carbonate buffer (pH 9.6) 100 µL was dropped and incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. The wells were washed for three times with 1 % w/v BSA (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). The various concentrations of standard neomycin solution (0–20 ng mL−1) were diluted with 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The competitive reaction was mixed 50 µL of each solution between neomycin in various concentrations (or samples) and CDs-Abneo. The mixture was dropped into each well on microplate and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. Then, the microplate was washed with washing buffer and tapped dry. The captured CDs was measured by intensity of fluorescence signals to read excitation at 360, and maximum emission at 450 nm, the decreasing of fluorescence intensity was compared with against the concentration of neomycin. The developed competitive CDs-LISA procedure was shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A competitive enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA)
ce:section.ce:para: This method was adapted from a previous report (Teepoo et al., 2019). The coating antigen neomycin-BSA was added in microplate and followed 100 µL of 0.1 moL L−1 carbonate buffer (pH 9.6). After that, the microplate was incubated at 4 °C for 12 h, then that was washed for three times by 1 % w/v BSA (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). After dialysis the anti-neomycin, the labeling of anti-neomycin with biotin were calculated the final concentration 100 µg L−1 to obtain biotinylated anti-neomycin. Briefly, 10 mg L−1 BAC-Sulfo NHS and anti-neomycin solution were mixed and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at 4 °C for 24 h and mixed with 1 % w/v BSA (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). The competitive reaction was dropped 100 µL of both, the various concentrations antigen neomycin with the range for 0–200 ng mL−1 (or sample) and biotinylated anti-neomycin in plates at 37 °C for 40 min. Then also washed for three times again as above procedure. Afterward, HRP-streptavidin solution (0.001 mg mL−1) was added and washed by 0.05 % Tween 20 (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). The substrate solution was filled 100 µL for containing TMB and H2O2 into plate and after 15 min was then dropped 0.5 moL L−1 H2SO4 to stop the reaction. The resulting yellow solutions were measured by UV–visible spectrophotometer at wavelength of 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical validation of the developed assay
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method has been validated according to the Eurachem Guide second edition 2014 (Magnusson and Örnemark, 2014). Several parameters were investigated under the optimum conditions to evaluated the analytical performances of the developed assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity range, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) and precision
ce:section.ce:para: A linear range was obtained from the calibration curve, which was plotted between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of standard neomycin (0–20 ng mL⁻¹). For LOD and LOQ, the data were calculated according to 3 SD/m and 10 SD/m, respectively, where m was the slope of the calibration curves and SD was the standard deviation of the 10 different blank signal responses. The precision of the proposed assay was evaluated by the relative standard deviation (%RSD) for seven replicates of neomycin detection at the concentration level of 0.2 ng mL⁻¹ .

To verify the selectivity of the proposed assay, the potential interfering antibiotics that are similar to neomycin (streptomycin, gentamicin, penicillin, and tetracycline) were investigated. Measurements were performed at the concentration of antibiotics of 0.2 ng mL⁻¹ , and the fluorescence intensities were then determined to evaluate the selectivity of the developed competitive CDs-LISA for neomycin.

The procedures for preparing the real sample follow a previously reported method (Song et al., 2015). The four commercial milk samples were randomly selected (two raw milks, one pasteurized milk, and one sterilized milk) and purchased from a local market and convenience store in Pathum Thani, Thailand. After that, the defatted milk samples were freshly prepared by treatment with ethyl acetate. Briefly, the 10 mL of ethyl acetate and 10 mL of each milk were transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. The mixtures were treated with ultrasonic sonication for 30 min, and then the samples were centrifuged at 2000 r min⁻¹ for 20 min to separate the fat and protein in the upper layer. Finally, the supernatant was then evaporated to remove the ethyl acetate. Shortly after collection and storage at 4 °C, the treated milk samples were further analyzed for detection of neomycin residues by the proposed assay.

The CDs were synthesized by dissolving urea and citric acid in deionized water and subsequently subjecting the mixture to a hydrothermal treatment. In order to examine the properties of CDs, the functional groups, morphology, particle size and zeta potential were characterized. Fig. S1. shows the results of the FTIR analysis, where the CD spectra showed bands or distinct peaks at a specific wavelength. The vibrational stretching at 3404 cm−1 and 1678 cm−1 indicates the presence of O–H/N–H and CO bonds, confirming the presence of hydroxyl groups or amines within the CDs. Furthermore, the high adsorption peak at 1576 cm−1 corresponds to the bending of N–H bonds. These functional groups are also present in citric acid and urea, indicating their successful use in CDs preparation. The surface functional groups on the CDs, such as the —NH, —OH, and CO, play a crucial role in antibody coupling. The morphology and particle size characterization of the CDs by TEM exhibited spherical morphologies, with CDs particle sizes of approximately 6 nm ( Fig. 2). To describe the surface charge of the CDs, the zeta potential was examined using DLS. As seen in Fig. S2, the zeta potential of −18.5 indicated that the CDs contain a negative surface charge resulting from the presence of anionic groups that contribute to the total negative charge of the CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

CDs from one-step hydrothermal treatment were obtained the dark blue in term of solid ( Fig. 3, inset). The optical properties of the CDs were then illustrated, including the excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs, the specific absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CDs before and after antibody conjugation. As seen in Fig. S3, the fluorescence spectra of CDs at a range of 330–370 nm as excitation wavelength was no significant different trend and the strongest fluorescence emission (λem) was determined to 443 nm via fluorescence excitation at 360 nm. Fig. 3 shows the absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CDs, where the CDs provided the bright blue color under ultraviolet light (UV) of 365 nm and the blue-green color in white light (Fig. 3, inset). The two specific wavelength peaks for absorbance were observed at ∼240 and ∼340 nm, resulting from π-π* transition of CC bond of and n-π* transition of CO bond, respectively. Furthermore, the strong emission wavelength for the CDs was presented to be 443 nm based on the fluorescence emission scans from 370 to 600 nm with an excitation wavelength for 360 nm.

Next, the amine coupling reaction using EDC and NHS was performed to conjugate CDs and anti-neomycin (CDs-Abneo), in which carboxylic acid group on the CDs was activated for coupling with primary amine on the antibodies. The confirmation of the successful conjugation between the CDs and the antibodies is well presented by the UV–vis and fluorescence spectrum analysis. As a result of conjugation anti-neomycin with CDs, the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs-conjugated anti-neomycin (CDs-Abneo) exhibited the stronger absorbance band at 260 nm than that of the pure CDs, suggesting the existence of amino acids on the CDs-Abneo ( Fig. 4a).

Subsequently, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs-Abneo showed the decreased fluorescence intensity at 1795 and a slight red-shifted at 450 nm (∼7 nm shifted), compared to the original fluorescence spectrum of non-conjugation CDs (fluorescence intensity; 4300 and λem; 443 nm) (Fig. 4b). The decreased and red-shifted fluorescence spectrum may be caused by the increased size of CDs-Abneo after the proteinaceous layer on CDs. The observation confirmed the successful functionalized CDs via antibody conjugation (Disha et al., 2022; Thakur et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020). These results indicated that the synthesized CDs provided excellent optical properties, suggesting that the CDs are promising as a fluorescent probe in this proposed assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the competitive CDs-LISA for the detection of neomycin
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the successful conjugation of neomycin with BSA via EDC as an amide coupling reagent, the neomycin-BSA conjugate and BSA only were immobilized on the polystyrene well plates as an analyze competitor. The unbound neomycin-BSA conjugate or BSA were then washed using 1 % BSA (in 10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4). Next, the CDs-conjugated anti-neomycin (CDs-Abneo) were introduced into with (immobilized neomycin-BSA) and without (immobilized BSA only) neomycin-coated wells and the fluorescence intensities were investigated. As seen in Fig. 5, the fluorescence signal of immobilized neomycin-BSA exhibited 5 times higher than that of immobilized BSA only, indicating that the conjugation of neomycin with BSA was achieved resulting to capture by the CDs-Abneo as well. The feasibility of the proposed assay was then evaluated by incubating CDs-Abneo with the blank solution (10 mmoL L−1 phosphate buffer pH 7.4) and 0.2 ng mL−1 of neomycin solutions. After competition binding between free neomycin and neomycin-BSA for CDs-Abneo binding, the fluorescence intensities of neomycin in solutions showed competitive concentration-depending binding to the CDs-Abneo captured on the well plates, such that the fluorescence intensities decreased with the present of neomycin in solution. These results confirmed the feasibility of the proposed competitive assay for detection of neomycin.

There are many factors to achieve a highly sensitive competitive assay such as the concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate, incubation times, pH values and binding times. The amount of immobilized neomycin as an analyze competitor on the well plates plays the key role in the competitive assay. To optimize the amount of neomycin on the well plates, the concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate and incubation times for immobilizing on well plates were investigated. Firstly, the concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate from 0.1 to 1.2 mg mL−1 were immobilized into the well plates for 3 h to 24 h, and the fluorescence intensities were measured after incubation of CDs-Abneo. The concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate at 0.9 mg mL−1 provided the highest fluorescence intensity compared to the others concentration. Although, increasing of neomycin-BSA conjugate concentration higher than 0.9 mg mL−1 the fluorescence signal was not increased due to limited surface area of the well plates ( Fig. 6a). Similarly, the fluorescence intensities were gradually increased with increasing incubation times from 3 to 12 h, and decreased after 12 h resulted in steric hindrance between limited antigen coated on the well plates and excess antibody (Fig. 6b). Previous studies have also shown that the suitable incubation times of immobilized antibiotic-BSA conjugate on the well plates for competitive immunoassay were found to be 12 h (overnight) (Chen et al., 2007; Luo et al., 2016; Song et al., 2015; Teepoo et al., 2019). Upon increasing the immobilization time, the signal responses were not significantly different. Therefore, the optimized amount of neomycin as an analyzed competitor was obtained when the concentration of neomycin-BSA conjugate was 0.9 mg mL⁻¹ and incubation was 12 h.

As seen in Fig. 6c, the effect of pH on the signal responses of the proposed assay in different phosphate buffer pH was examined. The fluorescence signal gradually increased from acidic condition (pH 6) and reached the maximum fluorescence signal at pH 7.4, then decreased in alkaline environment (pH 8). This result might due to the influence of acidic and alkaline conditions to immunoreaction between antibody and antigen, and also the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases under strong conditions (Qi et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021). Next, the binding time for CDs-Abneo to neomycin coated in the well plates was investigated (Fig. 6d). The maximum fluorescence response was obtained at 20 min, and then reduced after 30 min, which indicated that the immunoreaction reached equilibrium at 20 min. Overall, our finding revealed that 12 h for incubation of 0.9 mg mL−1 neomycin-BSA conjugate and 20 min for binding time under the phosphate buffer pH 7.4 were selected as the optimized conditions for the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the competitive CDs-LISA for detection of neomycin
ce:section.ce:para:
The competitive CDs-LISA was developed for the determination of neomycin (Fig. 1a). Under the optimum conditions, the standard neomycin solutions and CDs-Abneo were simultaneously added into the coating neomycin in the well plates for competitive reaction. After incubation and washing, the fluorescence signals of the obtained CDs-Abneo in the well plates were determined. The fluorescence intensities were negatively correlated depending on the amount of concentration standard neomycin increased, resulted in the competitive reaction between standard neomycin solutions and coating neomycin in the well plates ( Fig. 7a). Then, the standard curve was plotted between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of standard neomycin (0–20 ng mL−1), it was collected at a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm with an excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The correlation coefficients R
2 value was 0.9931 and the linear relevant range from 0.02 to 2 ng mL−1 (Fig. 7b). Limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of neomycin for the proposed assay were calculated according to 3 SD/m and 10 SD/m, respectively, where m was the slope of the calibration curves and SD was the standard deviation of the blank signal response. The limit of detection was found to be 0.01 ng mL−1 (0.01 ppb) and the limit of quantification as 0.04 ng mL−1 (0.04 ppb).

To compare the sensitivity of the developed competitive CDs-LISA with traditional competitive ELISA, the corresponding absorbance at 450 nm of standard samples with various concentration of neomycin (0–200 ng mL−1) were measured. Then, calibration curve of the ELISA was performed and used to evaluate the performance of the new competitive CDs-LISA. Fig. S4 shows the calibration curve of competitive ELISA in linear range of 1–80 ng mL−1 and good linear correlation with the R
2 value of 0.9955. The sensitivity of the competitive ELISA was evaluated by LOD and LOQ, which were calculated with the same equations as above. The limit of detection for this assay was defined at 1.45 ng mL−1, and the limit of quantification was 4.6 ng mL−1. When compared to competitive CDs-LISA, it was evident that the sensitivity of competitive CDs-LISA (LOD; 0.01 ng mL−1) was more 100 time higher than that of traditional ELISA (LOD; 1450 ng mL−1). These results indicated that using CDs conjugate antibody was an effective key role to amplify the signal and increase the sensitivity of the developed competitive CDs-LISA for detection of neomycin (Song et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020). Moreover, the proposed assay shows the highest sensitivity compared with the previously reported of analytical-based material methods for neomycin detection, including liquid chromatography mass spectrometer (Msagati and Nindi, 2005; Tasci et al., 2021), electrochemical-aptasensors (Feng et al., 2019; Giang, 2021; Li et al., 2022; Lian et al., 2013), paper based-immunosensor (Wu et al., 2012), colorimetric and fluorometric sensors (Xiao et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2013), and SERS-based sensor (Karacaglar et al., 2020) ( Table 1). These results confirmed that the sensitivity of the developed assay was fully achieved via combination between excellent optical properties of CDs and high selectivity of antigen antibody interaction.

To investigate the precision of the proposed assay, the repeatability of the developed competitive CDs-LISA was evaluated based on results of neomycin detection at the concentration of 0.2 ng mL−1 for seven replicates and then calculated the relative standard deviation (%RSD) (Table S1). The reproducibility of the sensor was found to be acceptable (% RSD = 2.6). The results suggested that the proposed assay provided high precision.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed assay, 0.2 ng mL−1 of neomycin or other potentially interfering antibiotics, including streptomycin, gentamycin, penicillin and tetracycline and CDs-Abneo were simultaneously added into the well plates, and the fluorescence intensities were then measured to verify the selectivity of the developed competitive CDs-LISA for neomycin. The fluorescence intensities of the other antibiotics were significantly different with neomycin at a similar concentration, and the fluorescence signals were comparative to the blank solution (phosphate buffer), indicating that the proposed assay exhibited high selectivity for neomycin ( Fig. 8a). The fluorescence signals of streptomycin and gentamycin were almost unaffected even there are the same aminoglycoside class to neomycin, indicating that the higher neomycin selectivity of the competitive CDs-LISA was achieved by specific immunoreaction between CDs-Abneo and neomycin. Moreover, the high selectivity of this assay can be attributed to the significant differences in the number and structure of aminosugars linked by glycosidic bonds to a cyclitol component of aminoglycoside antibiotics (Stead, 2000). Compared to the previous studies, the great specificity of neomycin to the proposed competitive CDs-LISA was comparable to that of several competitive immunoassays (Chen et al., 2007; Haasnoot et al., 1999; Jin et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2011). Summarily, the specificity of the proposed assay demonstrates significant potential for the analysis of real milk samples.

Then, the storage stability of the proposed assay was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity of 0.2 ng mL−1 neomycin for neomycin-BSA conjugate immobilized on the well plates after storage at 4 ◦C for 90 d. After storage for 60 d, the fluorescence intensity remained at over 95 % of the initial value (day 1) (Fig. 8b), whereas the response decreased to 86.4 % of the initial value after 90 d of storage. Thus, these results suggested that the proposed assay exhibited good long-term stability for the detection of neomycin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of neomycin residues in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the feasibility of this method, four different milk samples (raw milk A, raw milk B, pasteurized milk, and sterilized milk) were purchased from a local market and convenience store in Pathum Thani, Thailand, and the competitive CDs-LISA were used to detect and quantify residues of neomycin. We performed the traditional competitive ELISA as a comparative and reference method. Table 2 presents the concentrations of neomycin residues in the real milk samples. In raw milk A, there is a good agreement between two methods of neomycin residue detection with no significant difference (t-test analysis). However, only the developed competitive CDs-LISA can detect the neomycin residues in pasteurized milk (0.23 µg kg−1), resulting from its very low detection limit (LOD: 0.01 µg kg−1). These findings indicated that the competitive CDs-LISA was more sensitive than that of traditional competitive ELISA. In addition, the concentrations of neomycin residues in all milk samples did not exceed the MRLs, confirming the milk samples' good quality.

In order to assess the precision of this method, experiments were conducted to determine the amount of neomycin present in milk samples by adding known amounts of neomycin standards (0.1 and 0.5 µg kg−1) to the samples. Table 3 demonstrates that the average recoveries of the proposed assay for neomycin in milk samples ranged from 84.7 % to 113.2 %, with a relative standard deviation ranging from 3.36 % to 10.67 %. These findings indicate that the proposed assay was a reliable, precise, highly sensitive, and efficient technique for detecting neomycin residues in milk samples. The new competitive CDs-LISA and competitive ELISA (Table S2) demonstrated a high recovery rate with a good relative standard deviation (%RSD), in comparison to other competitive ELISA methods used to find neomycin residues in milk samples ( Table 4). The recovery results were the same as those from other competitive ELISA studies (Chen et al., 2007; Giang and Dao, 2020; Haasnoot et al., 1999; Jin et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2011). However, the developed competitive CDs-LISA is an inexpensive and uncomplicated synthetic approach that eliminates the need for many incubations and washing processes, as demonstrated in standard competitive ELISA. This reduction in steps allows for a detection time of only 0.5 hours. In addition, the LOD of the developed assay exhibited great lower than those found in other previously competitive ELISA methods. Thus, the advantages of the proposed competitive CDs-LISA compared to other methods are a quick test with high sensitivity.

Remarkably, our newly created competitive CDs-LISA detection of neomycin residues in milk samples demonstrates more advantages than the traditional competitive ELISA in terms of simplicity, sensitivity, analysis time and cost-effectiveness. Moreover, the CDs probe used for this assay can be obtained easily by the one-step hydrothermal synthesis method compared to the standard ELISA using an enzyme as a bioprobe. Therefore, the proposed assay could be a useful way to find neomycin residues in milk samples, which would improve dairy product safety monitoring.

In this study, we demonstrated a competitive carbon dot-linked immunosorbent assay (CDs-LISA) that uses a CDs-conjugated antibody as a nano-fluorescent probe to increase the fluorescence signal response, allowing for rapid and sensitive detection of neomycin residue in milk. The fluorescent CDs were synthesized in a single step hydrothermal process and utilized to conjugate neomycin antibodies via amide coupling. Following the competition binding reaction, the fluorescence intensities demonstrated competitive concentration-dependent binding to the CDs-Abneo recorded on the well plates, with fluorescence intensities steadily decreasing as the neomycin concentration increased. The present assay provided high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 0.01 ng mL−1 and high selectivity for neomycin detection compared to other interferent antibiotics. The sensitivity of the assay was comparable to the previously reported competitive ELISA methods for the detection of neomycin residues in milk samples, with more rapid detection within 30 min. Furthermore, milk samples treated with neomycin showed a high recovery rate (84.7 %–113.2 %) with an acceptable %RSD (3.36–10.67 %). The challenge remains in enhancing the stability of the optical characteristics of CDs via surface modification. In conclusion, the developed competitive CDs-LISA method provides fast detection of neomycin residues in milk with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability using fluorescent CDs as a fluorescent reporter. Future efforts are in progress to identify multiple antibiotics in dairy foods using multi-colored CDs with competitive CDs-LISA systems.
